🟡 Hudson Estuary Resilience Effort

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Supports regenerative transition through a guild-based approach focused on food, shelter, ecology, and culture, working across the Hudson River estuary region to foster local collaboration, fund place-based solutions, and steward long-term bioregional resilience.

Bioregional Context

The Hudson River Estuary stretches 5,312 square miles from New York City to Troy, a tidal freshwater region hosting rare biodiversity, vibrant communities, and cultural history, but facing threats from pollution, housing crises, and climate impacts like flooding and sea level rise.

Place
New York, NY, United States
links
https://youtu.be/YPJ2Thbgbdc

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving ⭕ Community Governance ✍🏾 Investment_Vehicles 🔀 Grants_Programs 🛠️ Resource_Hub 🧪 Citizen_or_Community_Science 🎨 Art_and_Storytelling 🎉 Congressing_and_Events

Background

Lenape tradition names part of this estuary Mawa—a meeting place. The Hudson is a tidal freshwater wedge where Adirondack runoff meets Atlantic salt: twice daily the current reverses. It is also a social crossroads—Appalachian uplands, farms and cities, global diplomacy at the river’s mouth.

The estuary covers 5,312 square miles from upper Manhattan to Troy—HERE’s home ground for shared bioregional work. Most of the basin lies in New York, with extensions into Connecticut, New Jersey, and Massachusetts. It holds roughly 200 fish species, 19 rare-bird types, 140 rare plants, and 11 significant biodiversity areas—and figures in the early U.S. conservation story. The Appalachian Trail’s 2,000-mile route crosses the region, drawing long-distance hikers each year.

Pressures include pollution affecting drinking water for more than 100,000 people, combined sewer overflows, flood and sea-level risk, housing stress, invasives, and—less visible to many—rising forest-fire exposure in the Northeast.

Origins and Network

The Hudson Estuary Resilience Effort (HERE) grew out of Partners for Climate Action Hudson Valley. For roughly five years, that collaboration concentrated grants on four interlinked domains—ecology, food, culture, and shelter—testing how place-based funding could track cross-cutting resilience in the estuary. HERE carries that guild-shaped logic forward as its own organising frame.

Activities

🔸

HERE Magazine

 🔸

Bioregional Congress

Vision and Legacy

HERE treats the tidal Hudson as both ecology and social infrastructure: guilds, fund, and narrative media as ways to align regeneration with the communities who live here.

The aim is organising and capital that stay near the problem—here, in the estuary’s “meeting place.” A timeline ties guild development, fund-building, and congress to a single transition strategy: local action linked to regional-scale resilience.
